E2Idm8UAAAAJ.keywords: Design, Product, Education, Engineering, Assistive Technology, Accessible HCI, Rehabilitation Technology, Inclusive STEM Education, Engineering Design Education, VR/AR for Rehabilitation
E2Idm8UAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Mahender Mandala is a researcher affiliated with the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, working at the intersection of *design*, *engineering education*, and *accessible technology*. His work spans interdisciplinary teams and labs addressing human-centered product development and rehabilitation technologies. Central to his agenda are investigations into <u>inclusive design</u>, <u>assistive and rehabilitation technologies</u>, and <u>design pedagogy</u>, with a particular emphasis on understanding sociotechnical interactions in everyday and clinical contexts. His publications combine empirical studies, design interventions, and educational innovations to advance how products and curricula better serve diverse users, including people with disabilities and learners across the K–12 to veteran-to-teacher pipeline.

---

## Research Areas
Mandala’s corpus reflects sustained contributions to several interconnected research areas. In human–computer interaction and disability studies he explores theoretical and praxis-oriented questions (notably in "Articulations toward a crip HCI"), interrogating how sociotechnical articulation shapes accessibility and participation. In rehabilitation and health technology he studies adoption and usability of therapeutic technologies and exergames for motor recovery (see "Perceptions of stroke survivors regarding factors affecting adoption of technology and exergames for rehabilitation") and clinical outcome prediction related to mobility metrics (e.g., AM-PAC 6-Clicks work). In engineering and design education he investigates peer feedback mechanisms and scalable instructional practices for large classes ("Impact of collaborative team peer review on the quality of feedback in engineering design projects", "Comparison of Collective Team and Individual Student Peer Feedback on Design"), and develops accessible computing curricula and tools for K–12 learners with sensory and visual impairments ("Accessible computer science for k-12 students with hearing impairments"; "Accessible block-based programming for k-12 students who are blind or low vision"). Across these topics he combines qualitative and quantitative methods, design-based interventions, and community-engaged programs (e.g., service learning with veterans and teachers) to bridge research, practice, and policy in design and product development.

## Notable Works
- "Articulations toward a crip HCI" (2021) — A theoretical and critical contribution that frames HCI as sociotechnical articulation work, foregrounding disability perspectives and arguing for design approaches that center accessibility, lived experience, and infrastructural labor within technology development.
- "Perceptions of stroke survivors regarding factors affecting adoption of technology and exergames for rehabilitation" (2023) — An empirical study probing user-centered barriers and facilitators to adopting VR/AR-based exergames and other therapeutic interfaces, informing design recommendations for rehabilitation technologies that align with clinical and user needs.
- "Impact of collaborative team peer review on the quality of feedback in engineering design projects" (2018) — A pedagogical investigation demonstrating how structured, collaborative peer review can scale meaningful feedback in design courses, with implications for curriculum design, assessment, and instructor workload in engineering education.

## Academic Background
Mahender Mandala’s publication record and collaborations indicate a strong grounding in engineering design, rehabilitation science, and inclusive STEM education. Affiliated with the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, he has collaborated on projects and programs that intersect with departments and centers focused on rehabilitation technology and design (earlier work shows ties to initiatives similar to those at the University of Pittsburgh’s Rehabilitation Science and Technology programs). His projects have engaged NSF-style experiential learning programs (e.g., veteran/teacher research experiences) and multidisciplinary teams involving clinicians, educators, and technologists. With a body of work totaling approximately **142 citations**, Mandala is recognized for bringing together *design practice*, *accessibility*, and *educational innovation* to produce applied research and translational programs that benefit learners, clinicians, and people with disabilities.

E6h84HUAAAAJ.keywords: Cardiac dynamics, Complex systems, Nonlinear dynamics, Mathematical biology, Cardiac electrophysiology, Spiral and scroll waves, Cardiac arrhythmia, Action potential modeling, Alternans, Multiscale computational cardiac simulation
E6h84HUAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Elizabeth M. Cherry is a professor-level researcher based at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, leading a computational cardiac dynamics research group (hosted at http://thevirtualheart.org/) that focuses on the *mathematical and computational study of cardiac electrophysiology*. Her work sits at the intersection of **mathematical biology**, **nonlinear dynamics**, and high-performance computing, and emphasizes mechanistic, multi-scale models of the heart that connect ion-channel and cellular dynamics to tissue- and organ-scale behavior. Central themes in her laboratory include the study and control of reentrant waves and spiral/scroll wave dynamics, development of efficient simulation methods (including GPU and adaptive-mesh techniques), and translation toward <u>patient-specific</u> and <u>clinically relevant</u> simulations.

---

## Research Areas
Cherry's research spans several tightly connected areas: the biophysically based mathematical modeling of cardiac cells and tissue, analysis of complex spatiotemporal patterns (such as spiral and scroll waves) that underlie arrhythmias, and the development of computational and control strategies for arrhythmia termination. Her influential reviews and methodological papers (e.g., "Models of cardiac tissue electrophysiology: progress, challenges and open questions" and "Models of cardiac cell") synthesize multi-scale modeling approaches and place model development in the context of international initiatives such as the Cardiac Physiome Project and Virtual Physiological Human. She has advanced the dynamical-systems perspective on arrhythmogenesis (see "Multiple mechanisms of spiral wave breakup in a model of cardiac electrical activity"), quantified alternans, restitution, and memory effects in tissue ("Suppression of alternans and conduction blocks despite steep APD restitution"), and developed minimal and comparative ventricular models ("Minimal model for human ventricular action potentials in tissue", "A quantitative comparison of the behavior of human ventricular cardiac electrophysiology models in tissue"). On the computational side, Cherry has contributed space–time adaptive algorithms and GPU-accelerated approaches for real-time and large-scale simulation ("A space-time adaptive method for simulating complex cardiac dynamics", "Toward real-time simulation of cardiac dynamics"), and she has extended modeling toward translational applications including low-energy defibrillation strategies ("Low-energy control of electrical turbulence in the heart", "Termination of atrial fibrillation using pulsed low-energy far-field stimulation") and construction of virtual patient cohorts for personalized simulation ("Creation and application of virtual patient cohorts of heart models"). More recently she has bridged cardiac dynamics with machine learning and data-assimilation approaches for prediction and reconstruction of complex signals.

## Notable Works
- "Models of cardiac tissue electrophysiology: progress, challenges and open questions" (2011) — a widely cited synthesis that framed multi-scale, biophysical cardiac modeling for the Cardiac Physiome effort and highlighted open problems in connecting cellular and tissue scales.  
- "Multiple mechanisms of spiral wave breakup in a model of cardiac electrical activity" (2002) — a foundational dynamical-systems study characterizing mechanisms by which reentrant spiral waves destabilize and lead to fibrillatory states.  
- "Low-energy control of electrical turbulence in the heart" (2011) — a key contribution toward understanding and designing *low-energy* strategies to terminate complex spatiotemporal arrhythmias, linking theory, simulation, and potential therapeutic application.

## Academic Background
Elizabeth M. Cherry has a sustained record of contributions spanning nearly three decades, with work that integrates applied mathematics, physics, and computational physiology. Her publication record (total citations ≈ **7053**) indicates broad impact across mathematical biology and cardiac electrophysiology. She has been an active participant in international modeling consortia (e.g., the Cardiac Physiome and Virtual Physiological Human initiatives) and in interdisciplinary, funded efforts to advance education and computational infrastructure (including NSF-supported educational workshops and expeditions in computing described in her teaching and outreach publications). Her trajectory includes doctoral-level training in a quantitative discipline and subsequent research and faculty appointments that culminated in her current affiliation at the Georgia Institute of Technology, where she directs the Virtual Heart research activities, collaborates across experimental and clinical groups, and mentors students and postdoctoral researchers in computational cardiac dynamics.

EMYV8cEAAAAJ.keywords: Ubiquitous Computing, Battery-Free Systems, Wearables, Intermittent Computing, Energy Harvesting, Low-Power Machine Learning, Batteryless IoT, Intermittent System Software, Reliable Timekeeping, Privacy-Aware mHealth Wearables
EMYV8cEAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Josiah Hester is an Associate Professor in **Interactive Computing** and **Computer Science** at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research group that designs systems for ultra-low-power, long-lived sensing and wearable platforms. His lab focuses on the confluence of hardware and software for sustainable embedded systems, developing tools, runtimes, and devices that enable *practical deployment* of tiny, maintenance-free sensors. Central to his work are the <u>battery-free / energy-harvesting paradigm</u>, <u>intermittent (power-failure-resilient) computing</u>, and <u>low-power machine learning and sensing for wearables and ubiquitous computing</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Hester’s research spans systems, tools, and applications for the next generation of pervasive computing devices. He has made sustained contributions to the design and evaluation of **batteryless, energy-harvesting sensors** (e.g., *The Future of Sensing is Batteryless, Intermittent, and Awesome*), software runtimes and kernels that provide progress and timing guarantees under frequent power failures (e.g., *InK: Reactive Kernel for Tiny Batteryless Sensors*, *Timely execution on intermittently powered batteryless sensors*), and practical prototyping and evaluation infrastructures (*Flicker*, *Ekho*, *Realistic Simulation for Tiny Batteryless Sensors*). He also addresses the systems challenges needed to bring intelligence to these constrained platforms, including compiler and toolchain co-design for low-power ML accelerators (*hls4ml*), energy-aware platform architectures (*Protean*), communication and I/O strategies for intermittently powered devices (*Greentooth*, *Efficient and safe i/o operations for intermittent systems*), and mechanisms for reliable timekeeping and persistent state (*Persistent Clocks for Batteryless Sensing Devices*, *Reliable timekeeping for intermittent computing*). In parallel, Hester applies these systems to wearables and health sensing—designing multi-sensor devices and privacy-aware camera systems (*NeckSense*, *Amulet*, *FaceBit*, *HabitSense*)—and explores sustainability and circularity of ubiquitous computing (work on soil-powered computing, circularity, and the broader *Internet of Batteryless Things*).

---

## Notable Works
- hls4ml: An open-source codesign workflow to empower scientific low-power machine learning devices (2021) — introduced a practical hardware–software co-design flow that brings ML inference to energy-constrained scientific devices, lowering the barrier to deployable, low-power ML on specialized accelerators.
- The Future of Sensing is Batteryless, Intermittent, and Awesome (2017) — a highly cited manifesto and systems examination articulating the vision and core technical challenges of a sustainable, battery-free Internet of Things and setting the agenda for intermittent computing research.
- InK: Reactive Kernel for Tiny Batteryless Sensors (2018) / Timely execution on intermittently powered batteryless sensors (2017) — a suite of systems and runtime techniques enabling forward progress, timing guarantees, and developer-friendly abstractions for devices that experience frequent power failures.

---

## Academic Background
Josiah Hester is an established researcher in embedded systems and ubiquitous computing based at Georgia Tech’s School of Interactive Computing and the School of Computer Science. His body of work has been widely cited (over 3,300 citations) and spans interdisciplinary collaborations across embedded systems, human-centered computing, and mobile health. Hester is active in open-source and tool-building efforts (for example, releasing *hls4ml*, *Bfree*, and *Battery-free MakeCode*), and his group publishes at leading systems and HCI venues while also contributing datasets, prototypes, and emulation tools that accelerate reproducible research. For more details on his degrees, honors, team members, and sponsored projects, see his homepage: http://josiahhester.com/.

EYo_WkEAAAAJ.keywords: Robotics, Artificial Intelligence, Learning from Demonstration, Reinforcement Learning from Demonstration, Interactive Learning, Sim2Real Transfer, Human-Robot Interaction, Explainable AI, Robotic Manipulation and Grasping, Embodied AI

EtoOcLMAAAAJ.keywords: Applied Mathematics, Plasma Physics, High Performance Computing, Scientific Machine Learning, Magnetohydrodynamics (MHD), High-order WENO schemes, Symplectic/structure-preserving neural networks, Neural ODEs and long-time forecasting, Collisional–radiative modeling, Drift-kinetic / Fokker–Planck solvers

F0HhkJIAAAAJ.keywords: Electrical and Computer Engineering, Computing Diversity, Context-aware communication, Availability prediction, Ubiquitous/home computing, Collaborative augmented reality, Virtual pointers, Computer-supported collaborative learning, Retention and persistence in computing, Service-learning and outreach
F0HhkJIAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Kristine Nagel is a researcher affiliated with the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, working at the intersection of **human–computer interaction (HCI)**, **context-aware systems**, and *computing education and diversity*. Her body of work spans both technical and educational domains: from studies of domestic communication and <u>availability-aware</u> services to the design of collaborative and augmented reality interaction techniques, and curricular and programmatic interventions aimed at improving retention and engagement for *underrepresented students in computing*. Nagel collaborates with research groups focused on **context-mediated communication**, **collaborative augmented reality**, and initiatives that broaden participation in computing.

---

## Research Areas
Nagel’s scholarship integrates empirical HCI research, systems design, and computing education. In the area of context-aware interpersonal communication she has investigated predictors of presence and appropriate interruption in the home (e.g., studies on <u>home life availability</u> and context-mediated family intercoms), and translated those findings into design guidelines for availability indicators and family communication mediators. In collaborative and augmented reality systems she has examined the <u>design space of referencing</u> — how users create and interpret referential cues — and evaluated interaction techniques such as virtual pointers to support shared attention. On the education and diversity side, Nagel has developed and evaluated learning tools (e.g., the Design Discussion Area and SMILE) and practical interventions — including service-learning courses, programming boot camps, and participation in the **STARS Computing Corps** — aimed at increasing confidence, persistence, and community-building among underrepresented computing students.

## Notable Works
- "Predictors of availability in home life context-mediated communication" (2004) — Empirical study identifying household activities and contextual cues that predict when it is appropriate to interrupt someone at home; foundational for design of availability-aware family communication systems.  
- "The design discussion area: A collaborative learning tool in support of learning from problem-solving and design activities" (1999) — Description and evaluation of the DDA scaffolding for student reflection and discussion in design-based learning, advancing tool-based support for collaborative learning.  
- "Understanding the design space of referencing in collaborative augmented reality environments" (2007) — Analysis of referential mechanisms in AR collaboration and articulation of design considerations that inform the creation of effective shared-reference techniques (later informing evaluations of virtual pointer methods).

## Academic Background
Nagel’s publication record shows sustained engagement with both research-intensive institutions and teaching-focused colleges in Georgia. While currently affiliated with the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, her work includes projects and program development at institutions such as Georgia Gwinnett College (e.g., course-embedded research projects and retention-focused initiatives). Across her career she has combined empirical HCI studies with practical curricular design, contributed to national broadening-participation efforts (notably the STARS Computing Corps), and produced work that has been cited across HCI, CSCW, and computing-education communities (total citations ~413). Her research trajectory reflects a blend of technical expertise in **electrical and computer engineering** and a steady commitment to *improving access, persistence, and pedagogy in computing*.

G6kA1CMAAAAJ.keywords: Numerical Algorithms, Data Analysis, Visual Analytics, Parallel Computing, Nonnegative Matrix Factorization, Sparse Nonnegative Matrix Factorization, Nonnegative Tensor Factorization, Dimensionality Reduction, Graph Clustering, Topic Modeling
G6kA1CMAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Haesun Park is a **Regents' Professor of Computational Science and Engineering** at the *Georgia Institute of Technology*, where she leads a research group focused on the development of advanced numerical algorithms and tools for large-scale data analysis and visual analytics (see her homepage: http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~hpark). Her work centers on the theory and practice of <u>Nonnegative Matrix Factorization</u>, <u>dimension reduction</u>, and scalable algorithmic methods for high-dimensional and heterogeneous data; she combines expertise in **numerical algorithms**, **parallel computing**, and **interactive visual analytics** to produce methods that are both mathematically grounded and practically useful. Her scholarship has had broad impact across machine learning, data mining, bioinformatics, and visualization (total citations: **21,059**).

---

## Research Areas
Park's research spans algorithm design, theory, and applications for low-rank and structured factorizations and supervised/unsupervised dimension reduction. A central thread is <u>Nonnegative Matrix Factorization (NMF)</u> — both algorithmic advances (e.g., alternating nonnegativity-constrained least squares and active-set methods in "Nonnegative matrix factorization based on alternating nonnegativity constrained least squares and active set method" and "Fast nonnegative matrix factorization: An active-set-like method and comparisons") and modeling extensions (orthogonal and 3-factor formulations in "Orthogonal nonnegative matrix t-factorizations for clustering", symmetric NMF for graph clustering in "Symmetric nonnegative matrix factorization for graph clustering", and tensor generalizations in "Algorithms for nonnegative matrix and tensor factorizations: A unified view based on block coordinate descent framework"). She has investigated *sparsity* and interpretability for clustering and topic modeling ("Sparse nonnegative matrix factorization for clustering"; "Sparse non-negative matrix factorizations via alternating non-negativity-constrained least squares for microarray data analysis"), and she has applied these methods to text and document analysis, interactive topic modeling (e.g., "Utopian: User-driven topic modeling based on interactive nonnegative matrix factorization"), and visual analytics systems ("iVisClustering", "iVisClassifier", and work on Jigsaw). Complementary areas include supervised discriminant methods and generalized SVD approaches for undersampled problems ("Generalizing discriminant analysis using the generalized singular value decomposition"; related optimization criteria), robust linear algebra and structured total least norm formulations ("Total least norm formulation and solution for structured problems"; "Formulation and solution of structured total least norm problems"), and aspects of scalable and fault-tolerant numerical computation (early work such as "An analysis of algorithm-based fault tolerance techniques"). Applications of her methods appear in bioinformatics (microarray missing-value estimation), hyperspectral imaging, and other multidisciplinary domains.

## Notable Works
- "Orthogonal nonnegative matrix t-factorizations for clustering" (2006) — Developed and analyzed constrained 3-factor NMF formulations that extend standard NMF for improved clustering performance, providing conceptual foundations that influenced subsequent orthogonal and symmetric NMF work.  
- "Sparse nonnegative matrix factorization for clustering" (2008) — Established links between NMF and classical clustering objectives (e.g., K-means) and introduced sparsity-promoting formulations that improved interpretability and clustering accuracy in high-dimensional settings.  
- "Sparse non-negative matrix factorizations via alternating non-negativity-constrained least squares for microarray data analysis" (2007) — Advanced practical algorithms for sparse NMF using alternating nonnegativity-constrained least squares, demonstrating utility on biological data (microarrays) and helping to popularize constrained least-squares approaches for NMF.

## Academic Background
Haesun Park is a senior faculty member at the Georgia Institute of Technology, holding the distinguished title of **Regents' Professor of Computational Science and Engineering**, a recognition of sustained excellence in research, education, and service. Over decades she has built a cross-disciplinary program interfacing applied mathematics, computer science, and domain sciences (bioinformatics, imaging, text analytics), supervising numerous students and collaborators and contributing foundational algorithmic and methodological advances. Her prolific publication record—spanning core numerical linear algebra, optimization for constrained factorizations, scalable and fault-tolerant computation, and interactive visual analytics—has earned broad citation impact (over **21,000** citations) and adoption in both theory and applications. She remains active in collaborative projects across Georgia Tech and the broader community, maintaining an active research group and sharing software and results via her laboratory web page.

Lxv3W74AAAAJ.keywords: Scientific Machine Learning, Large-scale Optimization, Numerical Methods, Inverse Design in Optics, Metasurfaces, Topology Optimization, Physics-Informed Neural Networks, Neural Operators, PDE Surrogate Modeling, Computational Imaging

Ntekq2MAAAAJ.keywords: Human Computer Interaction, Inclusive Design, Assistive Technology, Workplace Accommodations, Universal Design, Augmentative and Alternative Communication, Sonification, Accessible Informal Learning Environments, Environmental Accessibility Assessment, Dementia Communication
Ntekq2MAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Carrie Bruce is a **Principal Research Scientist** at the *Georgia Institute of Technology*, where she contributes to interdisciplinary teams that bridge **Human–Computer Interaction**, **Inclusive Design**, and **Assistive Technology**. Her work is centrally focused on creating and evaluating practical interventions that improve participation and communication for people with disabilities across everyday settings — from the *workplace* to *informal learning environments* such as aquaria. Bruce’s scholarship emphasizes <u>accessible interaction</u>, <u>environmental and communication assessment</u>, and <u>universal design</u>, combining empirical evaluation, tool development, and design guidance to support evidence-based accommodation and inclusion.

---

## Research Areas
Bruce’s research spans several tightly related areas: the design and evaluation of non-visual and sound-based interfaces for dynamic exhibits (e.g., sonification strategies for aquaria), linguistic and corpus-based analysis of augmentative and alternative communication (AAC) users in workplace settings, and the development of assessment toolkits and frameworks for improving communication and physical environments for people with dementia and other conditions. Specific topics include real-time sonification and interactive soundscapes for visitors with vision impairment ("Aquarium sonification"; "Aquarium fugue"; "Designing interactive sonification for live aquarium exhibits"), corpus creation and multidimensional discourse analysis for AAC device design and workplace accommodation ("The AAC and non-AAC workplace corpus (ANAWC)"; "Linguistic characteristics of AAC discourse in the workplace"), and operationalizing the International Classification of Functioning (ICF) for rehabilitation and workplace assessment ("Rehabilitation and health assessment: applying ICF guidelines"; "Development of an evidence-based conceptual framework for workplace assessment"). Her recent work also addresses practical accessibility practices for prototyping and web accessibility tools, reflecting a continuum from low-level assistive technologies to organizational and environmental adaptations.

## Notable Works
- "Rehabilitation and health assessment: applying ICF guidelines" (2009) — a foundational contribution linking the ICF framework to practical rehabilitation assessment methods; this work has been influential in framing how function, participation, and environmental factors are measured and addressed in accommodation planning.
- "Aquarium sonification: Soundscapes for accessible dynamic informal learning environments" (2006) — an early and impactful study developing sound-based interpretation strategies and interactive sonification approaches to make dynamic exhibits accessible to visitors with vision impairments.
- "Linguistic characteristics of AAC discourse in the workplace" (2013) — a corpus-informed analysis that identifies distinctive co-occurrence and discourse patterns of AAC users on the job, informing vocabulary design, device customization, and workplace communication supports.

## Academic Background
Bruce’s publication record and institutional role indicate an interdisciplinary training and career situated at the intersection of *HCI*, *rehabilitation sciences*, and *communication disorders* (inferred from collaborations with speech-language pathologists and rehabilitation researchers). As a senior researcher at Georgia Tech she has participated in federally supported and multi-institutional projects (for example, work associated with the RERC-UD activities and the Georgia Tech Accessible Aquarium Project), leading applied research that informs practice in museums, long-term care, and workplace accommodation. Her scholarly impact—reflected in a body of work spanning empirical studies, toolkits, corpus resources, and design guidance—has garnered broad uptake across accessibility, rehabilitation, and museum informatics communities (total citations: 499), and she is regularly engaged in cross-disciplinary collaboration with clinicians, designers, and technologists to translate research into usable accommodations and universal design practices.

OLDMURQAAAAJ.keywords: Combinatorial Scientific Computing, High Performance Computing, Parallel Computing, Biomedical Informatics, Hypergraph Partitioning, Sparse Matrix Partitioning, Parallel Graph Algorithms, Dynamic Load Balancing, GPU and Heterogeneous Architectures, Computational Pathology
OLDMURQAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Ümit V. Çatalyürek is a **Professor** in **Computational Science and Engineering** at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research program at the intersection of **Combinatorial Scientific Computing**, **High Performance Computing**, and **Biomedical Informatics**. His work emphasizes the design and implementation of *scalable, practical* algorithms and software for irregular and data-intensive scientific computations. Central to his research are <u>hypergraph models for partitioning</u>, <u>parallel sparse matrix computations</u>, and <u>dynamic load balancing</u> — approaches he has applied across domains from HPC kernels and graph analytics to biomedical image analysis and genomics.

---

## Research Areas
Çatalyürek's research spans algorithm design, software systems, and interdisciplinary applications. He has developed and analyzed hypergraph-based models for decomposing sparse computational domains and minimizing communication in parallel sparse matrix-vector products (see "Hypergraph-partitioning-based decomposition for parallel sparse-matrix vector multiplication" and "A fine-grain hypergraph model for 2D decomposition of sparse matrices"), and advanced multilevel and distributed hypergraph partitioning methods ("Parallel hypergraph partitioning for scientific computing", "Hypergraph partitioning for multiple communication cost metrics"). He contributed to dynamic and adaptive repartitioning techniques for adaptive simulations ("Hypergraph-based dynamic load balancing for adaptive scientific computations", "A repartitioning hypergraph model for dynamic load balancing"). On parallel graph algorithms and large-scale analytics, he has produced scalable BFS and graph coloring methods ("A scalable distributed parallel breadth-first search algorithm on BlueGene/L", "A scalable parallel graph coloring algorithm for distributed memory computers") and streaming and incremental algorithms for k-core and centrality computations. Çatalyürek has also bridged HPC techniques with data- and image-driven biomedical problems — developing image-analysis pipelines and benchmarking tools for sequencing and metagenomics ("Benchmarking short sequence mapping tools", "A comparative analysis of biclustering algorithms for gene expression data", "Computer-aided prognosis of neuroblastoma on whole-slide images") — and engineered toolkits and distributed frameworks to support these workflows ("DataCutter", "The Zoltan and Isorropia parallel toolkits").

---

## Notable Works
- "Hypergraph-partitioning-based decomposition for parallel sparse-matrix vector multiplication" (2002) — Introduced hypergraph models that more accurately capture communication volume for SpMV decomposition than traditional graph models, fundamentally influencing partitioning strategies for irregular sparse computations and enabling more communication-efficient parallel solvers.
- "PaToH: a multilevel hypergraph partitioning tool, version 3.0" / "PaToH (Partitioning Tool for Hypergraphs)." — Delivered a high-impact, widely used multilevel hypergraph partitioner that operationalized theoretical hypergraph models into practical software, facilitating adoption across VLSI, sparse linear algebra, and parallel applications.
- "The Zoltan and Isorropia parallel toolkits for combinatorial scientific computing: Partitioning, ordering and coloring" (2012) — Consolidated scalable partitioning, load balancing, and graph tools into production-quality parallel libraries, supporting dynamic and adaptive scientific applications and becoming a foundation for engineering robust parallel workflows.

---

## Academic Background
Çatalyürek has a long-standing academic and research career reflected by a prolific publication record since the mid-1990s and a total citation count in the tens of thousands (≈13,892), indicating broad and sustained impact. As a faculty member at the Georgia Institute of Technology he directs research efforts that produce both theoretical advances and widely used software artifacts (e.g., PaToH, Zoltan/Isorropia). His work routinely appears at the interface of algorithm theory, high-performance implementation, and domain science (computational physics, bioinformatics, digital pathology, and more), and he has collaborated with large-scale computing centers and interdisciplinary teams to translate combinatorial and parallel techniques into practice. His career is marked by major software contributions, highly cited methodological papers, and leadership in applying combinatorial scientific computing to real-world, data-intensive problems.

OT79Y9UAAAAJ.keywords: Networking, Networked Services, Wireless and Mobile Networks, Multicast, Multimedia Communication, Delay-Tolerant Networks, Mobile Ad Hoc Networks, Network Virtualization, Edge Computing / Mobile Cloud, Video Streaming

PNalJ58AAAAJ.keywords: Human Computer Interaction, Computer Supported Cooperative Work, Ubiquitous Computing, Postcolonial Computing, Human-Robot Interaction, Smart Homes, Usable Security, Mobile Messaging, Global Software Development, In-the-Wild Field Studies
PNalJ58AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Rebecca E. Grinter is a Professor and Associate Dean in the College of Computing at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where she leads an active research group in *Human–Computer Interaction* and *Computer-Supported Cooperative Work*. Her work sits at the intersection of **ubiquitous computing**, social computing, and practical system design, examining how technologies are adopted, adapted, and sustained in everyday life. Across long-term field studies and design-oriented research she foregrounds <u>domestic and everyday computing</u>, <u>youth and mobile communication practices</u>, <u>global and organizational coordination</u>, and <u>usable security and human-robot interaction</u> as central themes.

---

## Research Areas
Grinter’s scholarship spans empirical HCI, CSCW, and ubicomp with a strong emphasis on in-the-wild studies and socio-technical analysis. She has probed how sensor-driven and networked technologies enter domestic spaces in work such as “At Home with Ubiquitous Computing: Seven Challenges” and investigations of Roomba ownership and personalization (e.g., ““My Roomba is Rambo” intimate home appliances”, “'Pimp My Roomba' designing for personalization”, and “Domestic robot ecology: An initial framework…”). Her social computing work examines youth communication practices in studies like “Instant messaging in teen life” and “y do tngrs luv 2 txt msg?”, revealing how design constraints and affordances shape coordination and identity among teens. In the domain of distributed software engineering and organizational coordination she has revisited and extended Conway’s law through empirical papers including “Splitting the organization and integrating the code: Conway's law revisited” and “An empirical study of global software development: distance and speed.” She has also contributed to usable security and deployment work (e.g., “Security in the wild…” and “Network-in-a-Box”), sensing and interaction design (“Making sense of sensing systems”), and methodological reflection on cross-cultural design and equity through influential pieces such as “Postcolonial computing: a lens on design and development” and “Intersectional HCI,” which bring critical perspectives into design research.

---

## Notable Works
- “Postcolonial computing: a lens on design and development” (2010) — Introduced a critical analytic orientation for HCI that foregrounds power, history, and cultural difference in the design and evaluation of technologies deployed across diverse contexts.  
- “At Home with Ubiquitous Computing: Seven Challenges” (2001) — Articulated foundational challenges for designing ubicomp in domestic environments and shaped subsequent empirical and design work on the smart home and everyday sensor systems.  
- “Instant messaging in teen life” (2002) — A seminal qualitative study documenting how instant messaging supports adolescent social life, coordination, and identity practices; helped define socio-technical approaches to youth and mobile communication research.

---

## Academic Background
Rebecca Grinter is a senior scholar in HCI and CSCW with a sustained publication record dating to the mid-1990s and a total citation footprint exceeding 20,000, reflecting broad influence across Ubicomp, social computing, human-robot interaction, and software engineering research communities. As a faculty member and academic leader at Georgia Tech she combines empirical field methods, design sensibilities, and critical frameworks to address real-world problems of adoption, coordination, and equity. Grinter has been active in cross-disciplinary collaborations, served on program committees and editorial venues in HCI and CSCW, and has secured competitive funding to support longitudinal, deployed studies. Her work has been incorporated into teaching, mentoring, and institutional service, shaping both scholarly directions and practical design guidance for researchers and practitioners.

Q_4d9N0AAAAJ.keywords: Computer Graphics, Robotics, Scientific Visualization, Biological Simulation, Geometry Processing, Surface Reconstruction, Texture Synthesis, Implicit Surfaces, Fluid Simulation, Sim-to-Real Transfer
Q_4d9N0AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Greg Turk is a Professor at **Georgia Tech** in the College of Computing, where he leads a research group focused on core problems in **computer graphics** and their intersections with **robotics** and *scientific visualization*. His lab pursues a blend of algorithmic geometry, physically based simulation, and data-driven synthesis, with particular emphasis on <u>surface representation and reconstruction</u>, <u>texture and pattern synthesis</u>, and <u>simulation-based modeling of fluids and deformable materials</u>. Turk's work spans from foundational methods for converting range scans into coherent polygonal models to modern efforts that bring simulation and learning together for robust robot control; his homepage is http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~turk/ and his publications have accrued over **24,000 citations**, reflecting sustained influence across multiple communities.

---

## Research Areas
Turk's research broadly addresses geometric modeling, texture synthesis, physical simulation, and the growing interface between simulation and machine learning for control. In geometric modeling and reconstruction he developed practical pipelines for merging scan data into watertight meshes (e.g., "Zippered polygon meshes from range images") and advanced implicit-surface representations such as the <u>multi-level partition of unity implicits</u> and *variational implicit surfaces* for robust surface fitting and interpolation. In texture and appearance modeling he helped establish example-based synthesis techniques—most notably *graph-cut* based stitching for image and video ("Graphcut textures") and extensions to synthesizing textures directly on curved surfaces ("Texture synthesis on surfaces", "Geometric texture synthesis"). His simulation work spans cloth, viscoelastic and multiphase materials, and coupled solid–fluid interactions (examples include "Rigid fluid", "Melting and flowing", and methods for reconstructing particle-based fluid surfaces), together with methods for remeshing, simplification, and topology-aware surface tracking ("Deforming meshes that split and merge", "Simplification envelopes"). More recently Turk has contributed to robotics and learning for control and sim-to-real transfer—publishing on universal policies and randomized simulation strategies ("Preparing for the unknown", "Robot learning from randomized simulations", "Sim-to-real transfer for biped locomotion", "Learning symmetric and low-energy locomotion")—bringing his expertise in simulation and model representation to bear on data-driven policy learning.

## Notable Works
- "Zippered polygon meshes from range images" (1994) — A highly cited foundational method for fusing multiple range scans into a single coherent polygonal mesh; seminal for practical 3D digitization and surface reconstruction pipelines.  
- "Graphcut textures: Image and video synthesis using graph cuts" (2003) — Introduced graph-cut seam optimization for patch-based texture synthesis, significantly improving visual continuity and being widely adopted in image and video synthesis tasks.  
- "Multi-level partition of unity implicits" (2005) — Presented a scalable implicit surface representation built from localized basis functions, enabling robust reconstruction and modeling from very large point sets and influencing later work on hierarchical implicit modeling.

## Academic Background
Greg Turk has been a prominent member of the Georgia Tech faculty for many years, holding the title of Professor in the College of Computing and directing a long-running research program that has trained numerous Ph.D. students and postdocs. Over a multi-decade career beginning in the late 1980s and continuing through today, he has published widely in venues such as SIGGRAPH, TOG, and major robotics and visualization conferences, earning a broad citation footprint across computer graphics, vision, simulation, and robotics. His work has been supported by governmental and industrial research grants and has contributed to standard practices in mesh processing, texture synthesis, and simulation-based policy learning; he has served the community through program committees and editorial duties associated with these fields. For a complete list of publications, students, and current projects, see his homepage: http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~turk/.

XjeIqxYAAAAJ.keywords: Parallel computing, Programming languages, Compilers, Program Analysis, Runtime Systems, Task parallelism, Data race detection, Concurrent programming models, DNN accelerator dataflow, GPGPU/CUDA
XjeIqxYAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Vivek Sarkar is a professor in the **School of Computer Science** at the *Georgia Institute of Technology*, where he leads a research group focused on scalable software and runtime support for parallel and heterogeneous systems. His work spans the design of **programming languages**, **compilers**, and **runtime systems** to enable productive and high-performance parallel computing. Across decades of contributions he has advanced both theory and practice in areas such as <u>task parallelism</u>, <u>data locality and movement</u>, and <u>datarace detection</u>, coupling language-level constructs to efficient compiler and runtime implementations.

---

## Research Areas
Sarkar’s research targets the full stack of parallel-system software: from high-level language constructs and memory models to compiler analyses and low-level runtime scheduling. He has driven work on *object-oriented parallel languages* (notably the **X10** language) and its successors, exploring constructs for locality, asynchrony, and determinism. In compilers and program analysis he has produced scalable techniques for register allocation (e.g., **linear scan**), loop and array transformations, and array SSA forms that assist parallelization. On runtime systems and schedulers he has contributed algorithms and frameworks for partitioning and scheduling parallel programs, locality-aware work-stealing (e.g., **SLAW**), and mechanisms for combining inter-node and intra-node parallelism. More recently his group has applied a *data-centric* perspective to modern accelerators, producing tools (e.g., **Maestro**) and analyses to reason about DNN dataflow mappings, reuse, and hardware cost. Across these topics he repeatedly addresses core problems such as <u>datarace detection</u>, <u>synchronization abstractions</u> (e.g., phasers), and <u>scalability for exascale systems</u>.

## Notable Works
- "X10: an object-oriented approach to non-uniform cluster computing" (2005) — Introduced the **X10** language design that integrates locality and asynchrony to make distributed and multi-core programming more productive while enabling high performance on clusters and multi-core systems.  
- "The Jalapeno virtual machine" (2000) and "The Jalapeno dynamic optimizing compiler for Java" (1999) — Designed and implemented **Jalapeño/Jikes** virtualization and dynamic compilation technologies that advanced JVM research on server-class performance, scalability, and open-source research platforms.  
- "Linear scan register allocation" (1999) — Proposed the **linear scan** algorithm for fast, global register allocation that trades minimal optimality loss for dramatically improved compilation speed, widely adopted in JITs and compilers for dynamic and ahead-of-time systems.

## Academic Background
Sarkar’s publication record stretches from the mid-1980s to the present, reflecting a sustained research program in parallel computing, compilers, and runtime systems. He is currently a tenured faculty member at *Georgia Tech* and has collaborated extensively with both academic groups (e.g., work that influenced Habanero-Java and other language projects) and industry research teams (notably contributions tied to the **Jalapeño/Jikes** virtual machine lineage and the RAW architecture/compiler efforts). His work has influenced research on **exascale software**, memory models, and accelerator-aware compilation, and is widely cited (over **22,000** total citations), indicating broad community impact. Over his career he has supervised and mentored many students and postdocs, contributed to open-source research infrastructure (e.g., Jikes RVM), and played visible roles in multi-institution research efforts addressing the software challenges of emerging parallel and heterogeneous architectures.

YOGOScoAAAAJ.keywords: High performance Computing, Data Science, Computational Biology, Combinatorial Scientific Computing, Genomics, Genome assembly, Sequence alignment, Parallel algorithms, Hardware acceleration, Pangenomics
YOGOScoAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Srinivas Aluru is a **Regents' Professor** and **Senior Associate Dean** in the College of Computing at **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group focused on the intersection of *high-performance computing* and *computational biology*. His work centers on developing scalable, architecture-aware algorithms and software for large-scale biological data analysis, combining techniques from systems, algorithms, and domain biology to address problems in <u>genome assembly and alignment</u>, <u>comparative genomics</u>, and <u>scalable algorithm design</u>. He maintains an active lab and online presence (homepage: https://www.cc.gatech.edu/~saluru/) and his scholarship spans foundational theory, practical software, and close collaboration with experimental genomics teams.

---

## Research Areas
Aluru's research sits at the confluence of **High-Performance Computing (HPC)**, *Data Science*, and **Computational Biology**, with strong emphasis on algorithmic scalability and architecture-aware implementations. His contributions cover core algorithm design for string and graph representations of genomes (e.g., space-efficient and linear-time construction of suffix arrays), parallel and distributed methods for sequence assembly and clustering (PCAP, PaCE, parallel de novo and transcriptome assemblers), and practical high-throughput tools for read mapping and preprocessing (architecture-aware acceleration of BWA-MEM, Chromap). He has advanced methods for error correction and long-read processing (surveys and evaluations of error-correction methods, and mapping long reads to large reference databases), developed fast whole-genome comparison and ANI analyses for microbial species delineation, and explored alignment to and acceleration on specialized hardware. Across these topics he regularly addresses both algorithmic complexity and real-world performance on modern multicore, distributed, and accelerator-equipped systems.

---

## Notable Works
- "High throughput ANI analysis of 90K prokaryotic genomes reveals clear species boundaries" (2018, 5291 citations) — a large-scale comparative genomics study and scalable ANI pipeline that provided decisive evidence for microbial species boundaries by enabling high-resolution, whole-genome similarity analysis across tens of thousands of genomes.
- "The B73 maize genome: complexity, diversity, and dynamics" (2009, 4922 citations) — a landmark contribution to plant genomics that produced an improved draft of the 2.3-gigabase maize genome, elucidating gene content, transposable element dynamics, and providing widely used genomic resources for crop and evolutionary biology.
- "Efficient architecture-aware acceleration of BWA-MEM for multicore systems" (2019, 3065 citations) — a systems- and architecture-focused work that significantly sped up a core short-read aligner through careful algorithmic redesign and low-level optimization for modern multicore processors, demonstrating the impact of HPC approaches on everyday bioinformatics pipelines.

---

## Academic Background
Srinivas Aluru is a senior faculty member in the College of Computing at Georgia Tech, holding the rank of **Regents' Professor** and serving as **Senior Associate Dean**, reflecting both distinguished scholarship and academic leadership. Over a multi-decade career he has published influential work since the late 1990s across parallel algorithms, scientific computing, and computational molecular biology, accumulating over **22,000 citations** and establishing a reputation for bridging theory and practice. His publication record includes foundational algorithmic contributions (suffix arrays, parallel sequence alignment, space-filling curve partitioning), large-scale genomic projects (maize genome assembly, ANI-based microbial taxonomy), and systems-level acceleration for sequencing workflows (BWA-MEM optimizations, Chromap). He has collaborated extensively with plant and microbial genomics groups and with HPC researchers, and his roles at Georgia Tech indicate sustained involvement in mentoring, curriculum development, and building computational biology and data science capacity at an institutional level.
